input: What event marked Hakoris’s eighth regnal year domestically?
expected_output: A significant internal rebellion that was suppressed by General Nakhtnebef.
context: Domestically, Hakoris’s eighth regnal year was marked by a significant internal rebellion that was suppressed by General Nakhtnebef. Despite continued Persian aggression, Hakoris maintained Egyptian sovereignty and ensured relative peace and prosperity within his borders. His reign coincided with a period of complex international relations, including peace between Persia and Sparta but intermittent warfare among Greek city-states. Egypt under Hakoris emerged as a significant regional power allied with Greek and Cypriot interests, maintaining stability and independence during a time of shifting alliances across the eastern Mediterranean.

Politically, Hakoris skillfully balanced diplomacy and military preparedness to preserve Egypt’s independence from Persia. During his reign, Egypt remained free from Persian control. He concluded an alliance with Athens and employed a large mercenary army composed of seasoned Greek veterans. The Athenian general Khabrias served as one of his key foreign allies, while the Egyptian general Nakhtnebef (later known as Nectanebo I) commanded the native troops. Hakoris also forged alliances with Cyprus and maintained diplomatic ties with other Greek states. In 386 BCE, while Persia and Greece were engaged in conflict, Artaxerxes II concluded peace with Greece and redirected his attention to Egypt. Persian forces invaded Egypt in 385 and again in 383 BCE but were successfully repelled by Hakoris’s renewed naval power and well-organized defense under his generals.

Hakoris’s reign was marked by both political stability and extensive building activity within Egypt. He initiated a widespread program of temple restoration and construction, continuing the monumental architectural traditions of his predecessors. Notably, Hakoris accomplished what earlier rulers had failed to achieve in his ambitious building projects and additions to the Temple of Amun at Karnak. He also commissioned numerous monuments throughout Egypt, though his tomb has never been found. His capital saw the construction of an extensive monument complex, reflecting renewed artistic and religious vitality during his rule.

Hakoris, also known as Khnemma’atré, Achoris, Hagor, or Hakor, was the third ruler of Egypt’s Twenty-ninth Dynasty, reigning from approximately 393 BCE until his death in 380 BCE, a period lasting about thirteen years. Some sources, however, date his reign slightly differently, from 392 to 379 BCE. He was not related to the royal family of Nephrites I, the founder of the dynasty. Following the death of Nephrites I, who originated from Sais and had established his capital at Mendes, Hakoris rebelled against Nephrites I’s designated heir, Psammetichus. After roughly a year of internal struggle, Hakoris seized the throne, dating his own reign from the death of Nephrites I. He named his son, also called Nephrites, as his successor and sought to uphold the political and cultural ideals of the dynasty.

Hakoris died in 380 BCE after thirteen years on the throne and was succeeded by his son Nephrites II, who ruled briefly for about four months before being overthrown and killed by the general Nakhtnebef, who assumed the throne as Nectanebo I, founding the Thirtieth Dynasty. Despite uncertainties in ancient sources over whether Hakoris was the second or third ruler of the Twenty-ninth Dynasty—arising from the overlapping rule of Psammuthis in 393 BCE—Hakoris is firmly remembered as the dynasty’s most successful and dynamic king. His reign is distinguished by its architectural achievements, effective diplomacy, and the preservation of Egypt’s independence against Persian domination.

Hakoris also forged alliances with Cyprus and maintained diplomatic ties with other Greek states. In 386 BCE, while Persia and Greece were engaged in conflict, Artaxerxes II concluded peace with Greece and redirected his attention to Egypt. Persian forces invaded Egypt in 385 and again in 383 BCE but were successfully repelled by Hakoris’s renewed naval power and well-organized defense under his generals.

Hakoris’s reign was marked by both political stability and extensive building activity within Egypt. He initiated a widespread program of temple restoration and construction, continuing the monumental architectural traditions of his predecessors. Notably, Hakoris accomplished what earlier rulers had failed to achieve in his ambitious building projects and additions to the Temple of Amun at Karnak. He also commissioned numerous monuments throughout Egypt, though his tomb has never been found. His capital saw the construction of an extensive monument complex, reflecting renewed artistic and religious vitality during his rule.

Hakoris, also known as Khnemma’atré, Achoris, Hagor, or Hakor, was the third ruler of Egypt’s Twenty-ninth Dynasty, reigning from approximately 393 BCE until his death in 380 BCE, a period lasting about thirteen years. Some sources, however, date his reign slightly differently, from 392 to 379 BCE. He was not related to the royal family of Nephrites I, the founder of the dynasty. Following the death of Nephrites I, who originated from Sais and had established his capital at Mendes, Hakoris rebelled against Nephrites I’s designated heir, Psammetichus. After roughly a year of internal struggle, Hakoris seized the throne, dating his own reign from the death of Nephrites I. He named his son, also called Nephrites, as his successor and sought to uphold the political and cultural ideals of the dynasty.

Hakoris died in 380 BCE after thirteen years on the throne and was succeeded by his son Nephrites II, who ruled briefly for about four months before being overthrown and killed by the general Nakhtnebef, who assumed the throne as Nectanebo I, founding the Thirtieth Dynasty. Despite uncertainties in ancient sources over whether Hakoris was the second or third ruler of the Twenty-ninth Dynasty—arising from the overlapping rule of Psammuthis in 393 BCE—Hakoris is firmly remembered as the dynasty’s most successful and dynamic king. His reign is distinguished by its architectural achievements, effective diplomacy, and the preservation of Egypt’s independence against Persian domination.
